narrative: As a Black male student applying to law school, your profile includes a Year 1 school decile of 3, which is below the typical 5.7 average, a notably lower Year 3 decile of 1 compared to the usual 5.5, a lower-middle family income bracket, an LSAT score of 31 against an average of about 37, an undergraduate GPA of 2.3 versus the standard 3.2, and plans to attend part-time, which is uncommon as most students go full-time. The admissions model, trained on past students' data to predict first-time bar exam success, estimated a 48% chance you'll fail the bar—high enough to trigger a rejection, as the school prioritizes applicants likely to pass on their first try and succeed in practice. The key driver pushing toward this failure prediction was your Year 3 academic decile, as being in the bottom tier of your school strongly signaled lower bar exam readiness compared to peers. On a more positive note, your family income bracket worked in your favor, nudging the model toward seeing you as more likely to pass, perhaps reflecting resilience often seen in similar backgrounds. Your LSAT score also helped somewhat, pulling the prediction slightly away from failure despite being below average. Overall, these top three factors—dominated by that challenging Year 3 performance—outweighed others in shaping the decision, placing you below typical admitted students who boast stronger late-stage grades. While part-time status and your undergraduate GPA factored in, they weren't the biggest influencers here. This isn't a judgment on your potential as a Black man pursuing law amid real barriers, but a data-driven call based on patterns from thousands of past students. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT to boost your score, strengthening your final-year academics if possible, or targeting schools with holistic reviews that value your unique story. Building bar prep experience through targeted courses could also help demonstrate readiness in future applications. With persistence, you can reshape this narrative and find the right path.
